titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Mabel Dodge Luhan and Family Papers
titlestmt.author: by Brooke McManus

publicationstmt.publisher: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

maintenanceagency.agencyname: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

This means that anyone may copy, modify, and distribute this descriptive metadata without restriction or permission. The CC0 license is explained in full on the Creative Commons website: CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. More information is available on our Help page.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Tuesday March 18, 2025 at 00:27

did.unittitle: Mabel Dodge Luhan and family papers

corpname.part: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

descriptivenote.p: In English, with a small amount of material in Arabic and Hebrew.

did.abstract: The collection contains correspondence, writings, printed material, photographs and other papers by or relating to American arts patron and writer Mabel Dodge Luhan, her son, government official and author John G. Evans, and his wife, author Claire Spencer.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: This collection is open for research.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Purchased from the James Arsenault & Company on the Alfred Z. Baker, Jr. Fund, 2019.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into three series: I. Mabel Dodge Luhan Papers, circa 1900-1951. II. John G. Evans Papers, 1916-1967. III. Claire Spencer Papers, circa 1910s-1930s.

bioghist.head: Mabel Dodge Luhan (1879-1962)
bioghist.p: Mabel Dodge Luhan was a major cultural figure in the United States in the first half of the 20th century. She is best known as a patron of the arts and writer, author of a multi-volume autobiographical work and books on D. H. Lawrence, Taos, and Taos area artists.

bioghist.head: John G. Evans (1902-1978)
bioghist.p: John Ganson Evans was an American government official and author. He was born in Buffalo, New York to Mabel Dodge Luhan and Karl Evans and attended Yale University in 1920-1921. Evans married his second wife, Claire Spencer, in 1933. From 1942 to 1943, Evans was assistant project director of the American concentration camp in Poston, Arizona known as the Colorado War Relocation Center. He served as general superintendent of the United Pueblos Agency (now Southern Pueblos Agency) in Albuquerque, New Mexico from 1944 to 1946. Evans subsequently worked as a foreign aid official, including positions as director of the Joint U.S.-Iran Commission on Rural Improvement, deputy director of United States Technical Cooperation Administration (TCA) mission to Iran in 1951-1952, and briefly, deputy director of the TCA mission to Israel in 1953. He was appointed chief of the Egypt-Jordan Division in the Foreign Operations Administration in Washington, D.C. in 1953 and chief of the Near East Division in 1955. Evans published two novels, Andrews' Harvest (1933) and Shadows Flying (1936).

bioghist.head: Claire Spencer (1899-1987)
bioghist.p: Claire Spencer Evans was born in Glasgow, Scotland and emigrated to the United States in 1915. She was a painter, actor, and author whose novels included Gallows' Orchard (1930), The Quick and the Dead (1932), and The Island (1935). After divorcing her first husband, publisher Harrison Smith, Spencer married John Evans in 1933. She had four children from both marriages.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Mabel Dodge Luhan and Family Papers. Yale Collection of American Literature, Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Collections are processed to a variety of levels, depending on the work necessary to make them usable, their perceived research value, the availability of staff, competing priorities, and whether or not further accruals are expected. The library attempts to provide a basic level of preservation and access for all collections, and does more extensive processing of higher priority collections as time and resources permit.

This collection received a basic level of processing, including rehousing and minimal organization. Information included in the Description of Papers note and Collection Contents section is drawn from information supplied with the collection and from an initial survey of the contents. Folder titles appearing in the contents list below are often based on those provided by the creator or previous custodian. Titles have not been verified against the contents of the folders in all cases. Otherwise, folder titles are supplied by staff during initial processing.
This finding aid may be updated periodically to account for new acquisitions to the collection and/or revisions in arrangement and description.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The collection consists of correspondence, writings, printed material, photographs and other papers by or relating to American arts patron and writer Mabel Dodge Luhan, her son, government official and author John G. Evans, and his wife, author Claire Spencer.

Mabel Dodge Luhan papers contain of a draft of her unpublished memoir "Mexico in 1930," correspondence, family photographs, and an agreement related to Villa Curonia, Luhan's home with her second husband, Edwin Dodge.

John G. Evans papers primarily document his career as a writer and government official. Included are drafts of his novels Andrews' Harvest and Shadows Flying and related correspondence from Dorothy Brett, Una and Robinson Jeffers, Blanche Knopf, Elsie Clews Parsons, and other friends and publishing figures. Material concerning Evans's work at the Poston concentration camp in Arizona during World War II consists of correspondence, memoranda, and a chart of the camp's administrative organization, created by anthropologist Edward H. Spicer. Letters from War Relocation Authority Director Dillon S. Myer and sociologist John Collier discuss strikes by incarcerated Japanese Americans at Poston and the Tule Lake concentration camp. Papers related to Evans's tenure as general superintendent of the United Pueblos Agency (now Southern Pueblos Agency) in Albuquerque, New Mexico include letters from Truxton Canyon Agency Superintendent John O. Crow (Cherokee, or Aniyvwiya), Commissioner of Indian Affairs William A. Brophy, and Department of the Interior Secretary Julius A. Krug. A small amount of material relates to a proposal by nine New Mexico Pueblos to the United Nations to provide grain to European and Asian nations suffering from postwar famine. Papers concerning Evans's work with American foreign assistance programs in Iran and Israel consist of correspondence, photographs, government and financial documents, and printed material and ephemera. Included are several photograph albums of Iran. The collection also contains a photograph of Evans by Ansel Adams and a photograph of two Taos men on horseback by John Collier, Jr.
Claire Spencer papers consist of drafts of published and unpublished novels, among them Gallows' Orchard and The Quick and the Dead , and a few photographs, including a portrait by Berenice Abbott.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: The Mabel Dodge Luhan and Family Papers is the physical property of the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University. Literary rights, including copyright, belong to the authors or their legal heirs and assigns. For further information, consult the appropriate curator.

relatedmaterial.head: Associated Materials
relatedmaterial.p: Mabel Dodge Luhan Papers (YCAL MSS 196). Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University.
Mabel Dodge Luhan Collection (YCAL MSS 197). Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University.

corpname.part: United States. Bureau of Indian Affairs. Southern Pueblos Agency

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials maintain order found at time of acquisition.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series contains drafts of Mabel Dodge Luhan's unpublished memoir "Mexico in 1930" and an account of Mexico by her husband Tony Luhan, as well as correspondence with Luhan's son John G. Evans. Also included are photographs of Luhan, her first husband Karl Evans, John G. Evans, Luhan's house in Taos, New Mexico, and her dog.

did.unittitle: "Whirling around Mexico" [working title for "Mexico in 1930"], chapters 4, 6-20, draft, autograph manuscript
Tony's account ["Mexico in 1930"], drafts, autograph manuscript and typescript
Mabel Dodge Luhan and John G. Evans correspondence
Draft agreement between Edwin Dodge and Sarah Reeder regarding Villa Curonia
James T. Babb letter to Mabel Dodge Luhan

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Acknowledging gift of Marsden Hartley's "Portrait of Berlin" to Yale University.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Photographers include Mildred Tolbert and George Platt Lynes.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials maintain order found at time of acquisition.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series consists of writings, correspondence, government papers, photographs, and printed material by or relating to John G. Evans. Writings contain drafts of his two published novels, Andrews' Harvest and Shadows Flying , as well as poems by Evans. Also included is a scrapbook related to Andrews' Harvest and correspondence with friends and publishers regarding Shadows Flying . The series additionally contains materials documenting his work at the Poston concentration camp and November 1942 strike by Japanese American incarcerees; Evans's resignation as superintendent of the United Pueblos Agency; his positions with the United States Technical Cooperation Administration missions to Iran and Israel; and his appointment to the newly established U.S. Foreign Operations Administration in Washington, D.C. Photographs include two albums depicting Iranian people, agriculture, and Evans at work in Iran, as well as a few portraits of Evans and Taos men.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes letters from playwright Lynn Riggs; publishers Blanche Knopf, Alfred Harcourt, and Thayer Hobson; poets Witter Bynner and Robinson Jeffers; anthropologist Elsie Clews Parsons; writer Mary Austin; music critic Philip Hale; newspaper editor Arthur Brisbane; arts patron Noël Sullivan; critic Louis Kronenberger; and journalist Hutchins Hapgood, who writes about an encounter with Ernest Hemingway. A few letters and telegrams concern the sale of the movie rights for Andrews' Harvest . The scrapbook also contains a number of newspaper and magazine clippings.

did.unittitle: Runyon's Paradise [working title for Shadows Flying ], typescript draft sent to Alfred Knopf
Runyon's Sister [working title for Shadow's Flying ], typescript draft sent to Wm Morrow & Co.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Correspondents include artist Dorothy Brett, family friend Una Jeffers, and Natalie Scott, an author, social worker, and Red Cross nurse.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes a few unrelated documents, including description of a farm in Maine and hand-drawn layout of a house.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Folio in which poems in Box 2, f. 19-20 were formerly enclosed.

did.unittitle: Poston concentration camp organizational chart by Edward H. Spicer

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Chart is primarily in English, with some terms in Japanese.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes letters from Puebloans Bartolo Montoya (Isleta) and Ernest M. Yasteya (Laguna); Dr. Manuel Gamio, director of the Instituto Indigenista Interamericano (in Spanish, with an English translation); Helen P. Bradley Henry; Department of Interior colleagues John O. Crow (Cherokee, or Aniyvwiya), William A. Brophy, Julius A. Krug, future Supreme Court Justice Abraham Fortas (then Undersecretary of the Interior), and Ward Shepard; and Roger Baldwin of the American Civil Liberties Union. Several clippings and letters concern the offer of corn and wheat by nine New Mexico Pueblos to the United Nations Relief and Rehabilitation Administration for European and East Asian nations affected by postwar famine. Also contains Evans's proposal to the Isleta people respecting selection of a governor, his statement to the Albuquerque Indian School and Santa Fe Indian School classes of 1946, and a copy of his resignation letter from the agency.

did.unittitle: "Tehran and overlapping material re Tel Aviv transfer"
Point Four / Technical Corporation Administration, Israel [and overlapping material regarding D.C. transfer]
"Tel Aviv - Invitations, gifts, notes of congratulations, etc."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Primarily Department of Interior memoranda and correspondence respecting Evans's 1944 appointment as superintendent of the United Pueblos Agency and his resignation in 1946; the Technical Cooperation Administration program in Israel; and other matters. Includes a biographical statement by Evans and letter from War Relocation Authority Director Dillon S. Myer respecting the Poston concentration camp strike.

did.unittitle: Foreign Operations Administration payroll slips, travel documents and vouchers
"The Journal of Stephen Leigh," draft, typescript, corrected
Unidentified autograph manuscript writings and notes on Gallows' Orchard in unknown hand

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Front cover is hand-painted (possibly acrylic).

did.unittitle: Iran photograph album - pages removed from

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Interior cover reads: "To John G. Evans from his friends in T.C.J."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes several photos from Israel.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Signed by Adams on the recto.

did.unittitle: Evans in costume at Isadora Duncan's dance school

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Signed by Evans on the recto.

did.unittitle: Evans and Eliseo Concha, Taos, New Mexico

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Concha was a Taos carpenter. "Edwin T. Evans' grandchildren" is written on the verso.

did.unittitle: Two unidentified Taos men on horseback by John Collier, Jr.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials maintain order found at time of acquisition.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series primarily consists of typescript drafts of Gallows' Orchard , The Quick and the Dead , unpublished works, and a few short stories by Claire Spencer, as well as a file of reviews for her novel The Island. Also included are a photograph of Spencer from a touring production of Good Gracious, Annabel and a portrait by Berenice Abbott, likely taken around the time Spencer published her first novel.

did.unittitle: The Quick and the Dead , draft, typescript, corrected
For No Man [working title of Stark Major ], draft, typescript, corrected
Landscape with Figures: A Novel , complete typescripts, corrected
Landscape with Figures: A Novel , partial drafts
Landscape with Figures: A Novel , partial drafts
Portrait of Spencer on tour in Good Gracious, Annabel by Hixon Connelly
Portrait of Spencer by Berenice Abbott

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Signed by Abbott on the recto.
